title: Apply: Introduction to the Constitutional Convention
description: Apply your understanding on the reasons for the Constitutional Convention in this set of multiple-choice questions.
topic_path: The Road to Reform, the Constitutional Convention, and the Battle for Ratification
Apply: Introduction to the Constitutional Convention

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt of James Monroe’s 1787 letter to Thomas Jefferson. In the letter, Monroe discusses a possible Constitutional Convention.**

The affairs of the federal government are, I believe, in the utmost confusion. The convention is an [[☃ definition 1]] that will produce a decisive effect. It will either recover us from our present embarrassments or complete our ruin; for I suspect that if what they recommend should be rejected this would be the case. But I trust that the presence of [General] Washington will have great weight in the body itself, so as to [[☃ definition 2]] & [[☃ definition 3]] the demon of party, & that the signature of his name to whatever act shall be the result of their deliberations will secure its passage thro' the union.

**According to Monroe, what was the goal of the Constitutional Convention?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: control people by causing them to feel great respect or awe

choices.content: To debate who should be the first president
To discourage the formation of political parties
To fix the current disorder in the national government
To maintain the Articles of Confederation

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt of James Monroe’s 1787 letter to Thomas Jefferson. In the letter, Monroe discusses a possible Constitutional Convention.**

The affairs of the federal government are, I believe, in the utmost confusion. The convention is an [[☃ definition 1]] that will produce a decisive effect. It will either recover us from our present embarrassments or complete our ruin; for I suspect that if what they recommend should be rejected this would be the case. But I trust that the presence of [General] Washington will have great weight in the body itself, so as to [[☃ definition 2]] & [[☃ definition 3]] the demon of party, & that the signature of his name to whatever act shall be the result of their deliberations will secure its passage thro' the union.

**What role did Monroe believe George Washington would play in the Constitutional Convention?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: control people by causing them to feel great respect or awe

choices.content: His immense popularity would encourage the delegates to keep the current Articles of Confederation intact.
He would encourage the delegates to debate issues until they reached the best conclusion possible.
His presence would get the other delegates to attend the convention, as people would want to meet him.
He would legitimize the recommendations of the convention and ensure that the public would accept it.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Alexander White’s 1788 article in the *Winchester Virginia Gazette*, written as the states were holding their ratification conventions.**

Can you then suspect the Federal Convention, the members of which stand fair in point of reputation, notwithstanding the most virulent abuse of party rage in the State where they sat. But when you remember among them was a Washington, whose hair became grey and eyes dim in watching over your safety, whose disinterested patriotism has raised him above the reach of [[☃ definition 1]]; and a Franklin, whose philosophical and political abilities have [[☃ definition 2]] him the admiration of the world. . . 

Is it possible that a Washington and a Franklin could conspire to enslave their country?

**This excerpt can be used as evidence to support which statement?**
[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Washington’s and Franklin’s presence at the Constitutional Convention helped make the convention legitimate in the eyes of Americans.
Washington and Franklin’s frequent disagreements at the Constitutional Convention led to a public reluctance to accept the new Constitution.
Washington and Franklin’s participation in the Constitutional Convention did not encourage Americans to accept the proposed Constitution.
Washington and Franklin’s role in the Constitutional Convention was minimal to ensure that all competing voices could be heard.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Alexander White’s 1788 article in the *Winchester Virginia Gazette*, written as the states were holding their ratification conventions.**

Can you then suspect the Federal Convention, the members of which stand fair in point of reputation, notwithstanding the most virulent abuse of party rage in the State where they sat. But when you remember among them was a Washington, whose hair became grey and eyes dim in watching over your safety, whose disinterested patriotism has raised him above the reach of [[☃ definition 1]]; and a Franklin, whose philosophical and political abilities have [[☃ definition 2]] him the admiration of the world. . . 

Is it possible that a Washington and a Franklin could conspire to enslave their country?

**What argument does White make in the article?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: That the ratification of the Constitution was an illegal political revolution that was meant to oppress the American people
That the ratification of the Constitution couldn’t be dangerous or harmful because Franklin and Washington helped create it
That even though Franklin and Washington did not participate in the Constitutional Convention, the Constitution was crafted with them in mind
That implementing the system described  Constitution did not mean that the entire structure of government was going to change

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from James Madison’s 1787 letter to Edmund Pendleton.**

The absence of one or two States however will not materially affect the deliberations of the [Constitutional] Convention. Disagreement in opinion among those present is much more likely to embarrass us. The nearer the crisis approaches, the more I tremble for the issue. The necessity of gaining the [[☃ definition 1]] of the convention in some system [of government] that will answer the purpose, the subsequent [[☃ definition 2]] of Congress, and the final [[☃ definition 3]] of the states, present a series of chances, which would inspire despair in any case where the alternative was less formidable.

**What did James Madison fear might happen at the Constitutional Convention?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: That not all of the states would send delegates
That the convention would not reach an agreement
That the American public would learn of the convention
That the delegates would want to keep the Articles of Confederation

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from James Madison’s 1787 letter to Edmund Pendleton.**

The absence of one or two States however will not materially affect the deliberations of the [Constitutional] Convention. Disagreement in opinion among those present is much more likely to embarrass us. The nearer the crisis approaches, the more I tremble for the issue. The necessity of gaining the [[☃ definition 1]] of the convention in some system [of government] that will answer the purpose, the subsequent [[☃ definition 2]] of Congress, and the final [[☃ definition 3]] of the states, present a series of chances, which would inspire despair in any case where the alternative was less formidable.

**According to James Madison, what was the goal of the convention?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: To ensure that the Articles of Confederation remained in place to keep the states in an alliance
To secure the rights of states and limit the power of Congress under the Articles of Confederation
To come to an agreement about a new system of government that Congress and the states would accept
To have delegates from every state contribute to the new constitution to ensure complete agreement

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Henry Knox’s 1787 letter to George Washington in the months leading up to the Constitutional Convention.**

It is the general wish that you should attend. It is conceived to be highly important to the success of the propositions of the convention.

The Mass of the people feel the inconveniences of the present government, and [[☃ definition 1]] wish for such [[☃ definition 2]] as would remedy them. The alterations must be effected by wisdom and agreement, or by force—this convention appears the only mean to effect the alterations peaceably. If that should be unattended by a proper weight of wisdom and character, so as to carry into [[☃ definition 3]] its propositions, we are to look to events, and force, for arms. Were you not then to attend the convention slander and malice might suggest that force would be the most agreeable mode of reform to you. When civil commotion rages, no purity of character nor services however exalted, can entirely shield from the shafts of [[☃ definition 4]].

**What is the purpose of Knox’s letter to George Washington?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: the act of carrying out a plan

choices.content: To persuade him to accept the position of president of the United States
To advise him  not to get involved in disputes over the U.S. system of government
To inform him about the flaws of the Articles of Confederation
To convince him to participate in the Constitutional Convention

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Henry Knox’s 1787 letter to George Washington in the months leading up to the Constitutional Convention.**

It is the general wish that you should attend. It is conceived to be highly important to the success of the propositions of the convention.

The Mass of the people feel the inconveniences of the present government, and [[☃ definition 1]] wish for such [[☃ definition 2]] as would remedy them. The alterations must be effected by wisdom and agreement, or by force—this convention appears the only mean to effect the alterations peaceably. If that should be unattended by a proper weight of wisdom and character, so as to carry into [[☃ definition 3]] its propositions, we are to look to events, and force, for arms. Were you not then to attend the convention slander and malice might suggest that force would be the most agreeable mode of reform to you. When civil commotion rages, no purity of character nor services however exalted, can entirely shield from the shafts of [[☃ definition 4]].

**What argument does Knox make in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: the act of carrying out a plan

choices.content: George Washington’s participation in the Constitutional Convention is necessary to make its recommendations acceptable to the public.
George Washington’s popularity will distract the delegates of the Constitutional Convention from coming to the best conclusions.
George Washington must support the ratification of the Constitution or the American public will never accept the new government.
George Washington cannot be seen supporting the Constitutional Convention, as the American public will think he is trying to seize power unlawfully.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Edmund Randolph’s 1786 letter to George Washington.**

By the enclosed act, you will readily discover that the Assembly are alarmed at the storms which threaten the United States. What our enemies have foretold, seems to be hastening to its accomplishment; and cannot be frustrated [[☃ definition 1]] by an instantaneous, [[☃ definition 2]], and steady union among the friends of the Federal Government. To you I need not press our present dangers. The inefficiency of Congress you have often felt in your official character; the increasing [[☃ definition 3]] of our associated [[☃ definition 4]] you hourly see; and a [[☃ definition 5]] would be, I know, to you a source of the deepest mortification.

I freely, then, [[☃ definition 6]] you to accept the unanimous appointment of the General Assembly to the Convention at Philadelphia. For the gloomy prospect still admits one ray of hope; that those who began, carried on, and [[☃ definition 7]] the revolution, can yet rescue America from the [[☃ definition 8]] ruin.

**Which statement best summarizes the fear that Randolph expresses in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: slowness; lack of interest or energy
division into separate parts or pieces

choices.content: The United States is facing threats of invasion from foreign powers and does not have the power to fight back.
The national government under the Articles of Confederation will be unable to keep the country unified.
The citizens of the United States will see any efforts to replace the Articles of Confederation as treason and refuse to accept them.
The Constitutional Convention will likely be unsuccessful at creating a new government that meets the needs of the entire country.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from a 1786 pamphlet by William Barton.**

A member of civil society makes a voluntary surrender of part of his natural rights, in a free government, to secure the remainder: in the same manner, does each state [[☃ definition 1]] some of its sovereign [[☃ definition 2]], in order that they may be exercised by the supreme council of the union, for the greater security and happiness of the whole. 

If the Articles of Confederation be examined, it will appear to be the spirit and intention of them, that congress should be invested with all such powers and authority as are necessary to give consistency and efficiency to federal measures, both with respect to the different states in the union, and to foreign countries; and, at the same time, that each state should reserve such rights of [[☃ definition 3]] as might be fully [[☃ definition 4]] to its own legislation and government. 

**According to Barton, the purpose of a convention to review the Articles of Confederation is to accomplish which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: rights given exclusively to a person or group

choices.content: To completely replace the Articles of Confederation, which have failed to create a well-functioning republic, and restructure the government
To reform the structure of government to give states more authority to check the powers of Congress
To create a single national government and abolish state governments, as they are unnecessary
To give Congress the powers it needs to address national problems, while ensuring that the states also have enough power

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from James Madison’s 1786 letter to George Washington.**

[I]t was the opinion of every [[☃ definition 1]] friend whom I consulted that your name could not be spared from the [[☃ definition 2]] to the Meeting in May in Philadelphia. It was supposed that in the first place, the peculiarity of the mission and its acknowledged [[☃ definition 3]] over every other public object, may possibly reconcile your undertaking it, with the respect which is justly due & which you wish to pay to the late officers of the army; and in the second place that although you should find that or any other consideration an obstacle to your attendance on the service, the advantage of having your name in the front of the appointment as a mark of the [[☃ definition 4]] of Virginia, and an invitation to the most select characters from every part of the Confederacy, ought at all events to be made use of.

**Which statement best expresses the purpose of Madison’s letter?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: To convince Washington to attend the Constitutional Convention
To persuade Washington to serve as the first president of the United States
To ask him to lead a military unit to suppress Shays’ Rebellion
To get the commonwealth of Virginia to ratify the Constitution

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Benjamin Rush’s “Address to the People of the United States” (1787).**

There is nothing more common than to [[☃ definition 1]] the terms of *the American revolution* with those of *the [[☃ definition 2]] American war.* The American war is over: but this is far from being the case with the American revolution. On the contrary, nothing but the first act of the great drama is closed. It remains yet to establish and perfect our new forms of government; and to prepare the principles, morals, and manners of our citizens, for these forms of government, after they are established and brought to perfection.

The [[☃ definition 3]], together with most of our state constitutions, were formed under very unfavourable circumstances. We had just emerged from a corrupted monarchy. Although we understood perfectly the principles of liberty, yet most of us were ignorant of the forms and combinations of power in republics. . . .

Most of the present difficulties of this country arise from the weakness and other defects of our governments.

**Which statement best summarizes Rush’s argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The calls for a convention to revise the Articles of Confederation will plunge the country back into war and undo the work of the Revolution.
The ratification of the Articles of Confederation will truly signal success for Americans, as it would form a new national government.
The American Revolution is not over, even though the fighting is done, as the people need to create a new, more effective government.
The American Revolution was a success, as Americans gained their independence from British rule and created a new government.
